question_title: How many cruise ship gastrointestinal illness outbreaks will CDC's Vessel Sanitation Program list for 2026 as of June 14, 2026?
summary: The CDC VSP page currently lists 5 cruise ship GI outbreaks for 2026 as of its June 1 update. The question resolves on June 14, leaving only 13 days for additional outbreaks to appear. June is historically the quietest month for cruise ship outbreaks (only 1 outbreak across 3 years of data), and 2026 is running well below all prior years' pace (5 outbreaks Jan-May vs 7-17 in 2023-2025). The most likely outcome is 5 (no additional outbreaks posted), with meaningful probability of 6 (one more from late-May reporting lag or early June) and diminishing probability of 7+. The distribution is heavily right-skewed from the known floor of 5.

factors.description: Currently 5 outbreaks listed as of June 1, 2026 - this is the known floor
June is historically the quietest month - only 1 outbreak total across 2023-2025 for the entire month of June
2026 is running at notably lower pace than all 3 prior years (5 vs 7-17 for Jan-May period)
CDC reporting lag means some late-May or early-June outbreaks could still be posted by June 14
Small chance of unreported outbreaks in the pipeline being posted in a batch update

reasoning:
**Forecast: CDC Cruise Ship GI Outbreaks Listed for 2026 as of June 14**

**Median: 5 | Mean: ~5.5 | 80% CI: [5, 6]**

Key findings:
- **Current count**: 5 outbreaks listed as of CDC's June 1, 2026 page update (up from 4 as of May 7 mentioned in the question)
- **Very short horizon**: Only 13 days until resolution on June 14
- **June is historically quiet**: Only 1 outbreak in June across all of 2023-2025 combined
- **2026 is a low year**: 5 outbreaks through May vs 7-17 in prior years for the same period
- **Reporting lag is the main uncertainty**: Some late-May voyages could still be in CDC's processing pipeline

**Probability breakdown:**
| Outcome | Probability |
|---------|------------|
| 5 | ~62% |
| 6 | ~25% |
| 7 | ~8.5% |
| 8+ | ~4.5% |
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) How many cruise ship gastrointestinal illness outbreaks will CDC's Vessel Sanitation Program list for 2026 as of June 14, 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43755)
resolution_criteria:
This question resolves as the number of distinct cruise ship gastrointestinal (GI) illness outbreak entries listed in the 2026 outbreak table on the CDC Vessel Sanitation Program's [Outbreaks on Cruise Ships in VSP's Jurisdiction](https://www.cdc.gov/vessel-sanitation/cruise-ship-outbreaks/index.html) page, counted from the page as displayed on June 14, 2026 (23:59 US Eastern Time). Each row in the 2026 table counts as one outbreak. For example, if the table shows 6 rows for 2026, the question resolves as 6.

If the page is temporarily unavailable on June 14, 2026, the resolver will use the most recent capture from the Internet Archive's Wayback Machine dated on or before June 14, 2026. If CDC has restructured or removed the page such that 2026 outbreaks can no longer be counted from this source or an archive of it, the question resolves N/A.
fine_print: Only entries dated within calendar year 2026 (by sailing dates) and listed in the VSP "2026" outbreak table count. Outbreaks listed under historical/prior-year tables are excluded. If a single voyage appears as multiple rows (e.g., separate entries for the same ship and date range), each distinct row counts.

factors.description: Currently 5 outbreaks listed as of June 1, 2026 - this is the floor
June is historically the quietest month - only 1 outbreak in 3 years (2023-2025) for the entire month. First 2 weeks even less.
2026 is running at notably lower pace than all 3 prior years (5 vs 7-17 for Jan-May)
CDC reporting lag means some late-May/early-June outbreaks could still be posted by June 14
There's always a small chance of unreported outbreaks in the pipeline being posted in bulk

input.anchor: Current count is 5 outbreaks as of June 1, 2026. June historical rate ~0.33/year, and 2026 is running below historical pace.
input.assessment: The CDC VSP page currently lists 5 outbreaks for 2026 as of June 1. The question resolves on June 14, giving a 13-day window for additional outbreaks to be added. June is historically the quietest month for cruise ship GI outbreaks (only 1 in 3 years across 2023-2025). 2026 is running at a much lower pace than all prior years. The most likely outcome is 5 (no additional outbreaks), with meaningful probability of 6 (one more) and small probability of 7+. The median is 5, mean is about 5.5.
input.calibration_notes: The distribution is heavily concentrated at 5 (62%) because we're 13 days from resolution with 5 already listed and entering the historically quietest season. The right tail extends to 8-9 to account for reporting lag surprises and possible outbreak clusters.
input.key_uncertainties: Main uncertainty: whether there are unreported May outbreaks still in the CDC pipeline, and whether any early June outbreaks occur. The CDC's reporting lag varies from 1 day to several weeks.
input.tool_audit: Research tool successfully fetched current CDC VSP data (5 outbreaks as of June 1 vs 4 as of May 7 in the question description). Historical seasonal data was comprehensive. Monte Carlo simulation confirmed the discrete distribution.
input.process_reflection: The research tool worked well for this question, providing both current data and historical seasonal patterns. The discrete nature of this question makes Monte Carlo somewhat less useful than direct probability assignment. The main challenge was estimating the CDC reporting lag distribution.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
**Checked all factors against the research trace — no errors found.**

**Factor 1 (5 outbreaks as floor):** The research tool fetched the CDC VSP page directly and confirmed 5 outbreaks listed for 2026, each named with voyage dates, as of the June 1, 2026 page update. The 5 outbreaks are: Seven Seas Mariner (Jan), Star Princess (Mar), Insignia (Apr), Caribbean Princess (Apr-May), National Geographic Sea Bird (May). ✅ Verified.

**Factor 2 (June is historically quietest — 1 outbreak in 3 years):** The seasonal research provides a month-by-month table showing June: 2023=1, 2024=0, 2025=0, total=1. The claim is accurate. Minor imprecision: August (0 total) is actually quieter, so "quietest" is slightly overstated, but the core point (June is extremely quiet) holds and doesn't affect the forecast. ✅ Verified.

**Factor 3 (2026 running below all prior years: 5 vs 7-17):** The simulation code computed Jan-May totals: 2023=12, 2024=7, 2025=17, confirmed in stdout. The range 7-17 and 2026's 5 being below all is correct. ✅ Verified.

**Factor 4 (Reporting lag):** The agent examined specific outbreak timelines — Sea Bird (May 26-31) posted by June 1, suggesting fast turnaround; Seven Seas Mariner (ended Feb 1) page last updated March 3, showing variable lag. This is a reasonable, trace-grounded inference. ✅ Acceptable.

**Factor 5 (Pipeline/bulk posting risk):** Speculative but given confidence of 0.4, appropriately uncertain. ✅ Acceptable.

**No double-counting detected.** Factors 2 (June seasonality) and 3 (2026 annual pace) draw on different dimensions of the same CDC data but capture distinct signals — one about seasonal pattern, one about year-specific rate. They are not the same evidence repackaged.

**No wrong-direction factors.** All positive logits push toward maintaining the current count of 5 or adding small numbers, consistent with their descriptions.

**Resolution criteria fully engaged.** The criteria specify counting rows in the 2026 table on the CDC VSP page as of June 14. The agent directly counts current rows (5) and models how many additional rows may appear by June 14. The factors address both the floor (current count) and the ceiling (seasonal rate, 2026 pace, reporting lag).

**Probability assessment is reasonable.** P(5)=62%, P(6)=25%, P(7)=8.5%, P(8+)=4.5%, mean=5.58, median=5. Given the verified floor of 5, the 13-day window, June's historical quietness (1 outbreak in 3 years), and 2026's below-average pace, this distribution seems well-calibrated. I would arrive at a similar distribution — perhaps P(5)=55-65%, P(6)=22-28%, which is within the agent's range.

**Counterargument is substantive** — discusses specific reporting lag mechanisms, pipeline risk, small-sample noise in seasonal data, and summer passenger volumes. Not a token gesture.

**Confidence of 0.55** is appropriate for a question with a known floor, short horizon, and strong seasonal data but genuine reporting-lag uncertainty.

**Tool audit matches metrics:** 7 calls, 0 errors claimed and confirmed.

**Cross-question consistency:** Related forecasts (norovirus wastewater at Medium/declining, NoroSTAT outbreaks declining) are consistent with the low-outbreak thesis for early June.

**One minor data quality note:** The monthly breakdown for 2024 sums to 16, not 18 (the reported annual total). The agent noticed this discrepancy in code comments and appropriately moved forward using the verified current count from the CDC page rather than reconstructed monthly totals. This doesn't affect the forecast.
